abstract: This paper by Fabio Giglietto examines the effects of Meta's policy to reduce the visibility of political content on Facebook, focusing on how this change affected Italian parliamentarians. The study appears to assess engagement and reach metrics for political figures' posts in the wake of the platform's algorithmic deprioritization of political content. No abstract was provided with the source, so further specifics on methodology and findings cannot be summarized here.
key_claims: Meta's policy to reduce political content visibility on Facebook has measurable consequences for political actors' reach.
Italian parliamentarians serve as a relevant case for studying the downstream effects of platform governance decisions on democratic communication.
methods: Case study focused on Italian parliamentarians' Facebook accounts.
Analysis of visibility and/or engagement metrics in relation to Meta's political content reduction policy (specific methodological details not available without the full text).
findings: Specific findings are not available from the provided material, as no abstract or body text was supplied.
contributions: Provides empirical evidence on how platform-level content moderation and ranking policies affect elected officials' public communication.
Adds an Italian and European case to a literature largely dominated by U.S.-centric platform studies.
Informs debates about platform accountability, algorithmic curation, and democratic visibility.
framing: The paper situates itself within platform governance and political communication research, engaging with debates over how algorithmic curation by major platforms like Meta shapes the visibility of political actors and discourse. The title's reference to a 'pretty blunt approach' signals a critical stance toward Meta's broad-brush policy of reducing political content.
